reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The CLI-vs-Console overview table at the top is immediately useful — it gives first-time users a mental model of the whole observability surface before reading anything else.
Every section clearly and consistently separates what you do in the CLI from what you do in the Console, which directly follows the section instructions.
Concrete CLI commands with realistic, non-trivial flags (e.g., --stat p99, --startTime 30m) give the page practical weight without requiring a full example stack.
The Issues section is tightly grounded in the Explanations.tsx source — the detection mechanics, CLI version requirement, two-step enablement, and safety limits all match the source accurately.
The FAQ covers all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, comparison/choosing) and the answers are concise enough to be useful as snippets.
Linking every section to its dedicated sub-page at the end keeps the overview scannable without burying detail here.
The 'CLI vs Console' comparison section at the bottom provides actionable workflow guidance that helps the first-time reader understand when to use each path.
problems: Section instruction violation: the page explicitly includes cost content in the body prose ('using AWS Kinesis Data Firehose (~$0.03/GB)') and in a full FAQ answer with specific CloudWatch pricing figures. The section instructions say: 'Cost is a separate section — do not include cost content here. Link to /managing-costs/* if cost context is needed.' Neither the ~$0.03/GB figure nor the per-GB/per-alarm CloudWatch prices appear in the provided source files.

The FAQ answer for 'How much does CloudWatch observability cost?' quotes specific AWS list prices (~$0.50/GB ingestion, ~$0.03/GB/month storage, $0.30/metric/month, ~$0.10/alarm/month) that are not present in any of the provided source files. The pricing rules say: 'Never invent or estimate AWS list prices that are not present in the provided source.'

'What Stacktape observability covers' is an H2 heading that reads like a sentence/question rather than a compact noun label. The style guide mandates 1-5 word noun-form H2s and flags headings that read like full sentences when a short label would work.

The log forwarding section states 'Kinesis Data Firehose retries delivery for a configurable duration (default: 300 seconds)' — this specific default is not grounded in any of the provided source files.

No recommended starting path for a first-time user. An overview page with this many surfaces should tell the reader 'if this is your first time, start with Logs — run debug:logs after your first deploy' or similar. Right now it presents all surfaces equally without guiding the beginner.

Some AWS jargon is used without explanation for a low-AWS-experience audience: 'CloudWatch Logs subscription filters', 'Kinesis Data Firehose'. These appear in body prose without a one-line explanation of what they are.
mandatoryFixes: Remove cost content from the body prose and redirect to /managing-costs/overview. Specifically: (1) remove '~$0.03/GB' from the log forwarding section heading area; (2) either remove the 'How much does CloudWatch observability cost?' FAQ entry entirely or replace its body with a 1-2 sentence pointer to /managing-costs/overview, per the explicit section instruction: 'Cost is a separate section — do not include cost content here. Link to /managing-costs/* if cost context is needed.'

Rename 'What Stacktape observability covers' H2 to a compact noun-form label — e.g. 'Observability surfaces' or simply 'Coverage'. The current heading reads like a sentence, which violates the 1-5 word noun-label guideline. Per the style guide: 'Penalize scannability by at least 1. Mandatory fix: Shorten section headings to compact labels; keep most H2s to 1-5 words.'

Remove or qualify the ungrounded 'default: 300 seconds' Kinesis Firehose retry claim in the log forwarding section — this specific default does not appear in any provided source file. Either remove the parenthetical entirely or replace with 'see AWS Kinesis Data Firehose documentation for retry configuration'.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence 'where to start' recommendation near the top, e.g. 'If this is your first deploy, run stacktape debug:logs to see your function output — that's the fastest way to confirm everything is working.' This gives the low-AWS beginner an immediate action rather than a feature survey.

Shorten 'CLI vs Console — choosing your workflow' H2 to 'CLI vs Console'. The em-dash structure makes it read more like a sentence title than a label; the content is good but the heading could be tighter.

Briefly gloss AWS-specific terms on first mention for the low-AWS-experience audience: 'CloudWatch Logs subscription filters (AWS's log routing mechanism)' and 'Kinesis Data Firehose (AWS's managed streaming delivery service)'. One parenthetical each is enough.

The issues section mentions 'supported workloads' but the Explanations.tsx source lists specific exclusions (user-managed packaging, edge functions, unsupported languages). Consider adding a one-line exclusion list ('detection is skipped for edge functions, user-managed packaging, and workloads with log forwarding enabled') so readers don't have to click through to discover their workload isn't covered.

The 'CLI vs Console' bullet lists are slightly redundant with content already covered in each surface section. Consider trimming to a single summary line per bullet rather than re-explaining the feature.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The summary table at the top is the page's best feature — it instantly maps all seven observability surfaces to their CLI command, Console view, and config property, so an engineer can orient themselves in under 10 seconds without reading prose.

The dedicated 'CLI vs Console' section clearly separates the two workflows with concrete, non-circular use cases for each. This directly satisfies the section instruction 'make it clear what the user can do from the CLI vs the Console' without duplicating it per-surface.
Shell command examples each get their own fenced block with no explanatory `#` comments inside — the copy-buffer trap is avoided throughout.

The alarms section correctly covers both the config-time `alarms` array (per-resource, version-controlled) and Console-managed alarm rules (organization-wide, applied at deploy time), including the `disabledGlobalAlarms` escape hatch.

The issues section is well-grounded in the Explanations.tsx source: fingerprinting, high-volume protection, subscription-filter slot conflict with log forwarding, CLI status management, and the 3.8.0 CLI version requirement all appear.
FAQ spans all three required question categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service, and comparison/choosing questions) and has 10 entries — quality is high and none feel padded.
problems: SECTION INSTRUCTION VIOLATION — Cost content appears inline despite the explicit section instruction: 'Cost is a separate section — do not include cost content here. Link to /managing-costs/* if cost context is needed.' Two violations: (1) the Log forwarding section states '~$0.03/GB' for Kinesis Firehose inline, and (2) the FAQ contains a full 'How much does CloudWatch observability cost?' entry with per-unit CloudWatch pricing ($0.50/GB ingestion, $0.03/GB storage, $0.30/metric, $0.10/alarm) that was almost certainly not grounded in the provided source files.

Issues section omits the false-positive risk documented explicitly in Explanations.tsx: 'it can also pick up application logs that merely look like errors.' This is a meaningful production caveat for teams who might over-trust the error inbox.

High-volume protection is described as 'built-in safety limits' (automatic), but Explanations.tsx reveals it is a user-configurable percentage setting in the Console: 'At 100%, every matching error event is processed. Lower percentages reduce detector work and Console reports during bursts, but occurrence counts become estimates and rare one-off errors are easier to miss.' Framing it as automatic limits rather than a tunable knob misleads operators.

Issues detection exclusions list is incomplete. The page states only 'log forwarding configured' triggers detection skip, but Explanations.tsx also lists user-managed packaging, edge functions, and unsupported languages as exclusions. An engineer with a custom-packaged Lambda reading this page would not know detection is skipped for their workload.

The subscription filter slot claim says 'each log group has a limited number of slots' — vague when the actual number matters. Explanations.tsx states specifically 'one of the two CloudWatch Logs subscription-filter slots on each monitored log group.' The concrete count of two is operationally significant.
mandatoryFixes: Remove all inline cost figures from the log forwarding section and from the FAQ, per the explicit section instruction 'Cost is a separate section — do not include cost content here.' Specifically: (1) delete '(~$0.03/GB)' from the log forwarding section opening sentence and replace with a link to /managing-costs/overview; (2) remove the FAQ entry 'How much does CloudWatch observability cost?' entirely, or replace it with a one-sentence answer that links to /managing-costs/overview without quoting per-unit prices. The Firehose and CloudWatch pricing numbers quoted in the FAQ are also not grounded in the provided source files, compounding the violation.
optionalImprovements: Add the false-positive caveat to the Issues section, directly from the source: 'Detection can also pick up application logs that merely look like errors. It is best for recurring runtime failures, not a complete observability or tracing system.'

Rewrite the high-volume protection paragraph to surface the user-configurable percentage knob: explain that the Console lets you set a sampling percentage, that at 100% every matching error is processed, and that lower values reduce detector invocations during bursts at the cost of estimated occurrence counts and potentially missed one-off errors.

Expand the detection exclusions list to the full set from Explanations.tsx: workloads with log forwarding configured, user-managed packaging, edge functions, unsupported languages, and disabled projects or stages.

Change 'each log group has a limited number of slots' to the specific count: 'each log group has two subscription filter slots.' The concrete number is what an engineer needs when deciding whether log forwarding and issue detection can coexist on a given workload.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.
The page is easy to scan: short H2s, a useful surface matrix, focused subsections, and a broad FAQ.

verifierResults.summary: The draft contains several Stacktape-specific claims that are broader or more detailed than the provided source supports. The most important fixes are to narrow global observability claims, avoid unsupported CLI/Console feature details, and remove unsupported implementation details for alarms, channels, notifications, log forwarding, and issue-management behavior.

issues.statement: “Every resource Stacktape deploys emits CloudWatch logs and metrics automatically.”
issues.reason: The provided source does not support a blanket claim that every deployed resource emits both logs and metrics automatically. The source only describes specific observability areas and gives examples of metrics monitored by alarm rules.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to what the source supports, for example: “Stacktape surfaces AWS metrics for resources such as Lambda functions, databases, and APIs, and the Console includes views for issues, alarms, alert channels, notifications, and alert history.”

evidence.quote: Alarm rules monitor AWS metrics for your resources — such as Lambda error rates, database CPU usage, or API latency.
Matching log events are sent to the Stacktape service lambda in your stack, parsed, fingerprinted, and reported back to the Console.

issues.statement: “Issues” can be managed from the CLI with `stacktape issues:list`, `issues:resolve`, `issues:ignore`, and `issues:reopen`.
issues.reason: The provided Console source explicitly says issues are managed from the Console. The navigation index confirms CLI pages exist, but no CLI source behavior is provided here, so the draft should not describe terminal management behavior as fact from these sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that Issues are managed from the Stacktape Console, and only link to CLI issue command pages if separate CLI source files are available for this page. Otherwise omit the CLI-management claims from the overview table and Issues section.

evidence.quote: Issues are managed from the Console. You can enable all projects, selected projects, and optionally only specific stage names. Choose All stages when every stage should report issues.

issues.statement: “Console alarm rules merge with per-resource alarms defined in config. Use `disabledGlobalAlarms` on a resource to opt out of specific Console-managed rules.”
issues.reason: The provided source supports Console alarm rules being saved and applied on the next deployment, but it does not support merge semantics, the `disabledGlobalAlarms` property, or opt-out behavior. Those are configuration-affecting claims and need type/source backing that is not present.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with the grounded behavior: “Creating or changing a Console alarm rule saves it in the Console; Stacktape creates, updates, or removes the underlying CloudWatch alarms and EventBridge notification routing the next time each matching project and stage is deployed.”

evidence.quote: Creating or changing a rule saves it in the Console. Stacktape creates, updates, or removes the actual CloudWatch alarms and EventBridge notification routing the next time each matching project and stage is deployed.

issues.statement: “Stacktape supports five channel types: Slack, Microsoft Teams, Email, Discord, Webhook,” with setup details for bot tokens, incoming webhooks, sender/recipient addresses, HMAC signing, and PagerDuty/Opsgenie use cases.
issues.reason: The source only gives examples of channels such as Slack, email, or webhook, and says channels can be used in notification rules, alarm rules, and budget alerts. It does not support the complete five-channel list or authentication/setup details.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the section to: “Channels are alert destinations such as Slack, email, or a webhook. Create channels in the Console, then use them in notification rules, alarm rules, and budget alerts.” Link to the alert channels page for exact provider setup.

evidence.quote: Channels are the destinations where alerts get delivered — for example, a Slack channel, an email address, or a webhook.
Create channels here, then use them in notification rules, alarm rules, and budget alerts to receive alerts where you need them.

issues.statement: “The supported integration types for deployment notifications are Slack, Microsoft Teams, and Email.”
issues.reason: The source confirms notification rules alert on deployment events and deliver to a channel, but it does not enumerate supported integration types for deployment notifications.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the integration-type list from the overview, or phrase it through alert channels generally: “Each notification rule delivers to a configured channel.”

evidence.quote: Notification rules let you get alerted when specific events happen — like a deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled.
Each rule specifies which events to watch and which channel to deliver the alert to.

issues.statement: “Stacktape supports three forwarding targets: Datadog, Highlight.io, Any HTTP endpoint” and “failed deliveries are backed up to an S3 bucket automatically.”
issues.reason: The provided source discusses log forwarding only indirectly as a reason issue detection may be skipped. It does not define forwarding targets, Firehose behavior, backup behavior, or provider-specific configuration types.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep only the grounded interaction with Issues: “On the next deployment, Stacktape skips issue detection for workloads with Stacktape log forwarding configured.” Move provider targets and delivery behavior to the dedicated log forwarding page only if its source files support them.

evidence.quote: On the next deployment, Stacktape skips issue detection for workloads with Stacktape log forwarding configured, user-managed packaging, edge functions, unsupported languages, and disabled projects or stages.

issues.statement: “No additional infrastructure is required — these services are provisioned automatically with your stack.”
issues.reason: For Issues, the source says Stacktape adds CloudWatch Logs subscription filters and uses a Stacktape service lambda in the stack. Saying no additional infrastructure is required is misleading because issue detection does require deployment wiring and a service Lambda/subscription filters.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with: “For Issues, Stacktape adds CloudWatch Logs subscription filters for supported workloads on deployment and sends matching events to the Stacktape service Lambda in your stack.”

evidence.quote: Older CLI versions will not add the CloudWatch Logs subscriptions needed for new stacks to report Issues.
Matching log events are sent to the Stacktape service lambda in your stack, parsed, fingerprinted, and reported back to the Console.

issues.statement: “Resolved issues reopen automatically if the error recurs. Ignored issues stay suppressed permanently.”
issues.reason: The source describes issue grouping, enabling, detection, safety limits, and that existing issues stay visible after monitoring is disabled. It does not describe reopen behavior or permanent suppression semantics for ignored issues.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the reopen/permanent suppression statements unless CLI or Console issue-status source files are added. Keep only grounded statements about grouping repeated runtime errors and managing Issues from the Console.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The summary table at the top (Surface / CLI / Console / Config) is excellent — a first-time user can immediately see what exists and where to find it, without reading every section.
CLI vs Console distinction is maintained throughout every section and synthesized in a dedicated summary section, directly satisfying the section instructions.
CLI command examples are concrete and copyable with realistic flags (`--startTime 30m`, `--stat p99`, `--filter "ERROR"`). Each is isolated in its own fenced block — no multi-command copy-trap.
FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service behavior, and choosing/comparison questions. The 10 entries are genuinely useful and not padded.
Issues section is well-grounded in the Explanations.tsx source, including the CLI v3.8.0 version gate, two-step activation model, fingerprint grouping, and subscription-filter slot limitation.
Log forwarding target table (Datadog / Highlight.io / HTTP endpoint) is clean and practical — readers can instantly see whether their preferred platform is supported.
Alert channels integration-type table with required config fields gives actionable detail without over-explaining.
The first paragraph is 51 words and self-contained — works as an AI Overview snippet.
problems: No 'where to start' guidance for a developer who just deployed their first stack. The page maps the entire observability landscape but never says 'after your first deploy, run this to see your logs'. The target persona explicitly wants a clear path from 'what is this' to 'I have it running' — this page stops at 'what is this'.

The Issues section is significantly more detailed than every other section on the page, creating an imbalance. Technical details about high-volume protection percentages, sample rates, bounded invocation counts, and subscription-filter slot mechanics belong on the dedicated Issues page, not the overview. This depth will overwhelm first-time users scanning the overview.

The FAQ entry 'How much does observability cost on AWS?' contains zero useful content — it just redirects to /managing-costs/overview. Even a single sentence ('CloudWatch charges per GB ingested and per metric alarm; most small stacks stay within the free tier') would make the answer actionable. A pure redirect is a low-value FAQ entry.

The Metrics table lists specific CloudWatch metric names (Invocations, Errors, Duration, Throttles, ConcurrentExecutions, CPUUtilization, etc.) as Stacktape resource capabilities, but the provided source file (Explanations.tsx) does not enumerate these metrics. These claims should be grounded in a CLI source or types file — the source-grounding verifier may flag this.

The 'Alarms' section asserts 'Resource types that support config-time alarms: Lambda functions, relational databases, HTTP API Gateways, application load balancers, and SQS queues' without a grounding source in the provided files. This list should be verifiable from types/stacktape-config/alarms.d.ts, which is not in the provided source set.
optionalImprovements: Add a short 'Quick start' note or Info callout near the top (after the table or as its own paragraph) pointing the first-time user to the single most useful first action: running `stacktape debug:logs` after deploying. One sentence is enough: 'If you just deployed your first stack, start with `stacktape debug:logs` — it shows everything your workload has written to CloudWatch since deployment.'

Trim the Issues section to a summary paragraph + a few key facts (what it detects, how to enable it, the CLI commands, the v3.8.0 requirement) and move the high-volume protection, sample-rate, and subscription-filter-slot details to the dedicated Issues page. The current depth is out of proportion with the other sections on an overview page.

Improve the cost FAQ answer with at least one concrete sentence about the free tier or pricing model (e.g., CloudWatch Logs free tier, alarm cost per month) so readers get useful context before being redirected. Per the section instruction, cost content belongs at /managing-costs/*, but the FAQ answer should still contain something readers can act on.
Consider renaming 'Observability surfaces' to 'What's available' or 'Overview' — the word 'surfaces' is slightly abstract for a first-time reader unfamiliar with the term.

Add a one-line note in the Metrics section acknowledging that custom application metrics (published via CloudWatch PutMetricData) can also be monitored — the current table implies only AWS-provided metrics are available, which may mislead readers building custom instrumentation.

The Alert history section mentions 'separate focused histories' (alarm history, notification history, budget history) but these are listed as sub-bullets of the unified view rather than getting brief description sentences. A one-sentence description per type would improve this section's standalone extractability for AI engines.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. The provided source file (alarms.d.ts) was not available for grounding — this list may be incomplete or wrong. The Explanations.tsx source explicitly names additional exclusions: user-managed packaging, edge functions, and unsupported languages. Issues section omits the configurable percentage-based high-volume sampling knob. Explanations.tsx explicitly describes it: 'At 100%, every matching error event is processed. Lower percentages reduce detector work and Console reports during bursts, but occurrence counts become estimates and rare one-off errors are easier to miss.' Metrics table listing available metrics per resource type (Invocations, Errors, Duration, Throttles, etc.) is not grounded in any provided source file. These are accurate CloudWatch-native facts, but presenting them as a Stacktape documentation table without caveat implies Stacktape enforces or restricts them, which it does not.

Expand the Issues section limitations list to include all source-documented exclusions: user-managed packaging, edge functions, and unsupported languages — not only log forwarding. A one-sentence bullet per exclusion is sufficient; engineers using custom Dockerfiles or edge Lambdas must know detection is skipped.

Add a sentence in the Issues section covering the percentage-based high-volume sampling control, e.g.: 'The Console also exposes a percentage-based sampling knob — at 100% every matching event is processed; lower values reduce load during error bursts at the cost of estimated occurrence counts and potential missed one-off errors.'

Remove the specific metric name enumeration from the Metrics table or move it to the dedicated /observability/metrics page. Replace with a one-liner like 'CloudWatch provides infrastructure metrics per resource type — see Metrics for the full list per resource.' reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page is easy to scan: short H2s, a useful surface-comparison table, and a broad FAQ with Stacktape, AWS-service, and choosing/comparison questions.

Correct the opening paragraph so it does not claim all observability surfaces use the same CloudWatch data or require no extra setup. Replace it with a narrower source-backed statement that Stacktape Console exposes issues, channels, notification rules, alarm rules, alarm state/history, and alert history, with issues using CloudWatch Logs subscriptions after deployment.

Revise the Alarms section to explicitly distinguish Console-managed alarm rules from config-time alarms using the actual config-time alarm source. If the config source is unavailable, remove the config-time resource-support list and per-resource `alarms` claims.

Remove unsupported CLI option details, metric lists, and Logs Insights behavior unless the corresponding CLI source files are provided. Keep only command names if they are sourced elsewhere, or mark the section as linking out rather than documenting behavior.

Rewrite the Log forwarding and Alert channels sections to match available sources, or add source files proving `logForwarding`, Kinesis Data Firehose, S3 backup behavior, supported targets, and integration field names.

Fix unsupported FAQ answers by replacing absolute claims with source-grounded wording. Do not state “No log data is lost” or “No additional infrastructure setup is required” unless source files prove those guarantees.
optionalImprovements: Add a short “Best first step” paragraph near the top: use CLI debug commands for immediate investigation, use Console surfaces for team monitoring and alert routing. Keep the wording grounded in CLI and Console sources.
Turn the CLI vs Console section into a compact table so readers can compare logs, metrics, alarms, and issues faster.
Where source files are added, include one copyable example per CLI surface and keep each command in its own fenced block, as the draft already does.

Consider adding a short “Limitations” section for source-backed constraints: issues are log-pattern based, issue wiring changes on next deployment, and issue detection is skipped for workloads with Stacktape log forwarding configured.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the supplied observability sources, especially around Issues, alarm rules, alert channels, notifications, and the documented CLI command names. The main problems are a few over-broad statements that treat all observability surfaces as CloudWatch-backed data views, narrow Issues enablement to project-only scope, and overstate alert-history delivery status and log-forwarding durability.

issues.statement: “Stacktape surfaces logs, metrics, alarms, issues, alert channels, notifications, and alert history through the CLI and Console — reading from the same AWS CloudWatch data your stack produces.”
issues.reason: The sentence overgeneralizes. Logs, metrics, alarms, and issue detection are CloudWatch-related, but alert channels are delivery destinations, notifications are deployment-event alerts, and alert history is a Console alert log rather than CloudWatch data produced by the stack.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the opening paragraph: say CloudWatch backs logs, metrics, alarms, and issue detection, while the Console also manages alert channels, deployment notifications, and alert history.

evidence.quote: Channels are the destinations where alerts get delivered
Notification rules let you get alerted when specific events happen
A unified log of all alerts across notifications, alarms, and budgets.

issues.statement: The draft describes Issues enablement as “Enabled per-project in Console” and says Issues work “beyond enabling it per-project in the Console.”
issues.reason: The Console source says Issues can be enabled for all projects, selected projects, and optionally specific stage names. Project-only wording is too narrow and omits stage scoping.
issues.suggestedFix: Write that Issues are managed in the Console and can be enabled for all projects, selected projects, and optionally specific stage names.

evidence.quote: You can enable all projects, selected projects, and optionally only specific stage names.

issues.statement: “The Console keeps a unified log of every alert sent across all surfaces — alarms, notifications, and budget alerts — with delivery status.”
issues.reason: The Console source supports a unified alert log, but it does not say the unified Alert History view includes delivery status. Delivery status is explicitly mentioned for focused notification and alarm histories, not for the unified alert-history explanation.
issues.suggestedFix: Say the Console keeps a unified log of alerts across notifications, alarms, and budgets. Then separately say alarm history and notification history include delivery status, if you want to keep that detail.

evidence.quote: A unified log of all alerts across notifications, alarms, and budgets.
including their delivery status
including their delivery status

issues.statement: “No log data is lost — you can reprocess from S3 later.”
issues.reason: The provided log-forwarding source supports automatic S3 backup for failed deliveries, but it does not support an absolute no-data-loss guarantee or a reprocessing guarantee. That stronger claim could mislead readers about failure modes.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with: “Failed delivery batches are backed up to S3, which gives you a recovery path for delivery failures.”

evidence.quote: Failed deliveries are backed up to an S3 bucket automatically.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly uses `stacktape debug:logs`, `stacktape debug:metrics`, and `stacktape debug:alarms` with valid documented routes.
The Issues section is strongly grounded in the Console source: CLI 3.8.0 wiring, subscription filters, supported languages, grouping, and limitations are represented accurately.
The draft avoids forbidden Stacktape config patterns; there are no hand-authored YAML config examples or fenced TypeScript Stacktape config blocks.
All internal markdown links point to routes present in the supplied documentation navigation index.
